summary: Pigpen‑heavy set with Attics, Lovelight & tight jam
blurb: The show delivers a Pigpen‑driven acoustic set highlighted by 'Attics Of My Life' and 'Turn On Your Lovelight', then erupts into an electric second half featuring a soaring 'Tight Up' jam. Jerry Garcia’s vocals and steel‑guitar work stand out, while the rhythm section provides a solid groove.
review: On July 12 1970 at the Fillmore East, the Dead opened with a midnight acoustic set that was all about Ron "Pigpen" McKernan’s soulful organ and gritty vocals. Highlights included a heartfelt rendition of “Attics Of My Life,” a raucous take on “It’s A Man’s World,” and an exuberant “Turn On Your Lovelight” that had the crowd dancing. After the New Riders’ interlude, Garcia switched to steel guitar before re‑joining the band for an electric second set that built into a joyous climax – the tight, upbeat jam that bridges into “The Other One” sequence and a celebratory encore of “Uncle John’s Band.” Reviewers note that while the audience tape is only fair in fidelity (hand claps, occasional muffling), the performance itself is vibrant and memorable, especially Pigpen’s commanding presence and Garcia’s energetic vocals.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Two of the three recordings are strongly positive, praising Pigpen‑heavy acoustic set, standout tracks like 'Attics Of My Life' and 'Turn On Your Lovelight', and an energetic electric second half. The third recording is mixed but only criticizes recording quality, not performance. Consistent praise across multiple sources yields a moderate‑high positive sentiment adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Most complete capture of the Pigpen‑driven set and electric second half; despite fair sound, it includes all standout moments praised by reviewers.

key_highlights: Pigpen’s powerhouse vocals and organ on “It’s A Man’s World” and “Turn On Your Lovelight”.
Acoustic gems: rare “How Long Blues” and moving “Attics Of My Life”.
High‑energy “Tight Up” jam that propels the second set into an expansive “The Other One”.
Recording is fair – audience tape needs EQ boost but retains raw live energy.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s vocals are gritty and energized; his steel‑guitar work with the New Riders is praised, and his presence throughout the show is described as dosed but lively.
